enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based accounting software from Sage Group for small businesses and sole traders, covering invoicing, expenses, cash flow, and tax-ready reporting.
enrichment.starting_price: From around $19 to $24 per month for the entry Start plan (promotional pricing varies)
enrichment.seo_title: Sage Accounting Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See Sage Accounting pricing, plans, and features for 2026, and learn how this Sage Group cloud accounting tool for small businesses stacks up.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sage Accounting, also marketed as Sage Business Cloud Accounting, is a cloud-based bookkeeping and invoicing platform built by Sage Group plc, a British multinational enterprise software company founded in 1981 by David Goldman, Paul Muller, and Graham Wylie in Newcastle upon Tyne, England, where the company remains headquartered today at its Cobalt Park campus. Sage Group is publicly listed on the London Stock Exchange and employs more than 10,000 people worldwide, serving several million small and medium-sized business customers across its accounting, payroll, HR, and ERP product lines. Sage Accounting is the entry-level, subscription-based accounting product in that portfolio, aimed at sole traders, freelancers, and small businesses, sitting below the desktop-oriented Sage 50cloud and the mid-market Sage Intacct and Sage X3 platforms used by larger organizations. The product is sold as a tiered monthly subscription, typically structured across an entry Start plan and higher Accounting and Accounting Plus tiers that progressively unlock features such as inventory tracking, multi-currency invoicing, cash flow forecasting, and more detailed financial reporting. Published US pricing has ranged from roughly 19 to 62 dollars per month depending on tier, with list pricing closer to 24 dollars per month once introductory promotions expire; Sage frequently runs limited-time offers such as several free months or steep percentage discounts for new customers, so the price actually paid varies by region, currency, and promotion timing. Core functionality includes automated bank feed reconciliation, customizable invoices with online payment collection, expense and receipt capture, sales tax or VAT calculation, cash flow forecasting, and standard financial reports like profit and loss and balance sheet statements, with multi-user access and role-based permissions available on higher tiers. Sage Accounting also connects into the broader Sage Business Cloud ecosystem, including Sage Payroll, and supports third-party integrations through Sage's app marketplace. It is most commonly compared against QuickBooks Online, Xero, FreshBooks, and Wave in the small-business accounting software category. Reviewers generally credit Sage Accounting with dependable core bookkeeping and invoicing tools, the backing of a long-established enterprise software vendor, and a wide network of accountant and bookkeeper partners familiar with Sage products, while common critiques include a less modern interface than some newer competitors, fewer built-in third-party integrations than QuickBooks or Xero, and pricing that becomes less transparent once promotional rates expire. For directory purposes, Sage Accounting belongs in the small-business accounting and bookkeeping software category, competing primarily on ease of use, pricing, and integration depth rather than on advanced ERP-grade functionality, which Sage reserves for its separate Intacct and X3 product lines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sage Accounting is the small-business tier of Sage Group's cloud accounting portfolio, sitting below Sage 50cloud and Sage Intacct in the company's product lineup. It targets sole traders, freelancers, and small teams that need straightforward invoicing, expense tracking, and tax-ready reporting without the complexity of a full ERP system.

Sage Group was founded in 1981 in Newcastle upon Tyne and has grown through decades of acquisitions and organic expansion into one of the UK's largest technology companies, giving Sage Accounting the backing of an established, publicly traded enterprise software vendor with a global customer base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform covers the essentials of small-business bookkeeping: bank feed reconciliation, customizable and payable-online invoices, expense and receipt capture, cash flow forecasting, and standard financial reports such as profit and loss and balance sheet statements. Higher tiers add inventory management, multi-currency support, and more advanced reporting.

Sage Accounting also connects into the broader Sage Business Cloud ecosystem, including Sage Payroll, and supports third-party integrations through Sage's app marketplace, letting growing businesses extend the platform rather than switching accounting systems entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sage Accounting is sold as a tiered monthly subscription, typically an entry Start plan and one or two higher Accounting and Accounting Plus tiers. US list pricing has started around 24 dollars per month, with promotional offers frequently bringing introductory rates as low as 19 dollars per month for new customers before reverting to standard pricing.

Exact pricing varies by country and active promotion, and Sage regularly runs discounts such as multiple free months for new sign-ups, so buyers should confirm current rates directly on Sage's regional pricing page before purchasing.

features.description: Automatically imports and matches bank transactions to keep the books current with minimal manual entry.

features.description: Photograph or upload receipts to log business expenses on the go.

features.description: Visual forecasting tools project incoming and outgoing cash to help avoid shortfalls.

features.title: Financial reporting
features.description: Standard profit and loss, balance sheet, and tax-ready reports for owners and accountants.

features.title: Multi-currency support
features.description: Available on higher tiers for invoicing and tracking transactions in foreign currencies.

features.title: Inventory tracking
features.description: Available on higher tiers to monitor stock levels tied to invoicing and purchasing.

features.title: Sage ecosystem integration
features.description: Connects to Sage Payroll and third-party apps through Sage's app marketplace.

pros: Backed by Sage Group, an established, publicly traded enterprise software vendor with decades of accounting-software experience
Straightforward invoicing and bank reconciliation suited to sole traders and small businesses
Tiered pricing lets businesses start cheap and upgrade features as they grow
Wide network of accountants and bookkeepers already familiar with Sage products
Regional tax and compliance features tuned for markets like the US and UK
cons: Interface viewed by some reviewers as less modern than newer competitors like Xero or QuickBooks Online
Fewer built-in third-party integrations than some rivals
Promotional pricing can make the effective long-term cost unclear until discounts expire
Advanced ERP-grade functionality requires stepping up to separate Sage Intacct or Sage X3 products
Some useful features, like inventory and multi-currency, are gated behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: What is Sage Accounting?
faqs.answer: Sage Accounting is cloud-based accounting software from Sage Group for sole traders and small businesses, covering invoicing, expense tracking, bank reconciliation, and financial reporting.

faqs.question: How much does Sage Accounting cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is tiered by month, with US list pricing typically starting around 24 dollars per month and promotional rates sometimes as low as 19 dollars per month for new customers.

faqs.question: Who makes Sage Accounting?
faqs.answer: Sage Accounting is made by Sage Group plc, a British enterprise software company founded in 1981 and headquartered in Newcastle upon Tyne, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Does Sage Accounting integrate with payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, it connects to Sage Payroll and other apps through Sage's Business Cloud ecosystem and app marketplace.

faqs.question: Is Sage Accounting good for freelancers?
faqs.answer: Yes, the entry Start tier is aimed at sole traders and freelancers who need basic invoicing and expense tracking.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sage Accounting and Sage Intacct?
faqs.answer: Sage Accounting is the small-business tier for basic bookkeeping, while Sage Intacct is a separate, more advanced cloud financial management platform aimed at mid-market and enterprise organizations.

faqs.question: Does Sage Accounting support multiple currencies?
faqs.answer: Multi-currency invoicing and tracking is available on the higher-priced Sage Accounting tiers.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Sage Accounting?
faqs.answer: Sage commonly offers free trial periods or promotional discounted months for new customers, though exact offers vary by region and time.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, GraphQL-first headless commerce platform for developers building custom storefronts, available self-hosted or as the managed Saleor Cloud SaaS.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open-core: free open-source self-hosted core, plus paid managed Saleor Cloud subscription tiers
enrichment.seo_title: Saleor Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Open-Source Commerce
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Saleor pricing, features, and open-source model explained. Compare self-hosted Saleor Core with managed Saleor Cloud plans for headless commerce.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Saleor is an open-source, GraphQL-first headless commerce platform built for developers who want full control over the storefront layer while relying on a composable, API-driven backend for products, checkout, orders, and payments. The technology traces back to 2013 as an internal e-commerce project of the Polish web agency Mirumee Software, and was formally spun out as its own startup, Saleor Commerce, in 2020 by co-founders Mirek Mencel and Patryk Zawadzki. The company's engineering team is based in Wroclaw, Poland, which serves as its headquarters, while marketing, sales, and customer success functions are primarily US-based and remote. Saleor Core, the open-source engine, is built on Python, Django, and GraphQL, is licensed under a BSD license that permits commercial use without licensing fees, and has attracted over 20,000 stars on GitHub, making it one of the most widely adopted open-source MACH (microservices, API-first, cloud-native, headless) commerce platforms. Notable brands reported to use Saleor include Lush and Breitling. Saleor has raised a total of roughly 10 to 11 million dollars in venture funding, including a 2.5 million dollar seed round from Berlin-based Cherry Ventures and an 8 million dollar seed-extension round led by Target Global with participation from e-commerce company Zalando, announced in February 2024. Commercially, Saleor follows an open-core model: businesses can self-host Saleor Core entirely for free, or pay for Saleor Cloud, the company's fully managed SaaS offering aimed at mid-to-large merchants that need automatic scaling, backups, and hosted infrastructure without operating Saleor themselves. Saleor Cloud pricing starts at 159 dollars per month for the Select plan, which includes up to 200,000 dollars in monthly gross merchandise value before additional usage fees apply, scaling up to a Volume plan at 399 dollars per month covering up to 1 million dollars in monthly GMV, with custom enterprise pricing above that. Because Saleor is headless, it separates the commerce backend from the customer-facing frontend, so merchants and agencies typically pair it with a separate frontend framework such as Next.js and build or customize a storefront rather than getting an out-of-the-box theme, which distinguishes it from monolithic platforms like Shopify or WooCommerce. This makes Saleor best suited to development teams and agencies building custom, high-performance storefronts or composable commerce stacks rather than non-technical merchants wanting a quick, no-code setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Saleor is a headless, API-first e-commerce platform built on Python, Django, and GraphQL. Its roots go back to 2013 as an internal project at the Polish agency Mirumee Software, and it was spun out as an independent company, Saleor Commerce, in 2020 by co-founders Mirek Mencel and Patryk Zawadzki, headquartered in Wroclaw, Poland.

The project has become one of the most widely adopted open-source MACH commerce platforms, with over 20,000 GitHub stars and reported use by brands including Lush and Breitling, and the company has raised roughly 10 to 11 million dollars from investors including Cherry Ventures, Target Global, and Zalando.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: As a headless platform, Saleor exposes commerce functionality such as product catalogs, checkout, orders, payments, and promotions through a GraphQL API, letting developers build fully custom storefronts on any frontend framework rather than relying on a fixed theme system.

Saleor Core is fully open source under a BSD license, meaning it can be self-hosted and modified for commercial use at no licensing cost, while Saleor Cloud provides a managed, auto-scaling hosted version for teams that do not want to operate their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Saleor Core is free, since the software is open source; costs in that path come from a team's own hosting, infrastructure, and development time rather than from Saleor itself.

For teams that prefer a managed service, Saleor Cloud starts at 159 dollars per month for the Select plan, covering up to 200,000 dollars in monthly gross merchandise value, with a Volume plan at 399 dollars per month for up to 1 million dollars in monthly GMV, and custom enterprise pricing for larger merchants.

features.title: GraphQL-first API
features.description: Full commerce functionality, from catalog to checkout, exposed through a single GraphQL API for flexible frontend development.

features.title: Headless architecture
features.description: Decouples the backend from the storefront so developers can build custom frontends in any framework.

features.title: Open-source core
features.description: Saleor Core is free and BSD-licensed, allowing self-hosting and commercial use without licensing fees.

features.title: Managed Saleor Cloud
features.description: A fully hosted, auto-scaling SaaS version of Saleor with backups for teams that do not want to self-host.

features.title: Composable commerce building blocks
features.description: Supports products, variants, checkout, orders, payments, and promotions as modular, API-accessible components.

features.title: Multi-channel and multi-currency support
features.description: Built-in support for selling across multiple sales channels, currencies, and regions from one backend.

features.title: Extensibility via apps and webhooks
features.description: Developers can extend functionality using Saleor Apps and webhook-based integrations without modifying core code.

pros: Fully open-source core with a permissive BSD license and no self-hosting licensing fees
GraphQL-first API gives developers precise, flexible access to commerce data
Backed by venture funding from known investors including Zalando and Target Global
Actively maintained with a large GitHub community and real-world adoption by known brands
Avoids vendor lock-in since the code can be self-hosted and modified
cons: Headless architecture requires development resources to build a custom storefront rather than an out-of-the-box theme
Not ideal for non-technical merchants wanting a quick, no-code setup
Saleor Cloud managed pricing scales with GMV, adding cost as a store grows
Self-hosting requires ongoing infrastructure management and Django/Python expertise
Smaller ecosystem of themes and plugins compared to mainstream platforms like Shopify

faqs.question: Is Saleor free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Saleor Core is open source under a BSD license and free to self-host and modify for commercial use. The managed Saleor Cloud service is a separate paid offering.

faqs.question: What is Saleor Cloud pricing?
faqs.answer: Saleor Cloud starts at 159 dollars per month for the Select plan (up to 200,000 dollars in monthly GMV), with a Volume plan at 399 dollars per month for up to 1 million dollars in monthly GMV.

faqs.question: Who founded Saleor?
faqs.answer: Saleor was formally founded as a company in 2020 by Mirek Mencel and Patryk Zawadzki, building on an internal project their agency Mirumee Software started in 2013.

faqs.question: Where is Saleor based?
faqs.answer: Saleor's engineering team and headquarters are in Wroclaw, Poland, with marketing, sales, and customer success primarily US-based and remote.

faqs.question: Is Saleor headless commerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Saleor is a headless, API-first commerce platform that separates the backend from the storefront, exposing functionality through GraphQL.

faqs.question: What technology is Saleor built on?
faqs.answer: Saleor Core is built on Python, Django, and GraphQL.

faqs.question: What companies use Saleor?
faqs.answer: Saleor has been used by brands including Lush and Breitling, among others, per public reporting.

faqs.question: How much funding has Saleor raised?
faqs.answer: Saleor has raised roughly 10 to 11 million dollars, including a 2.5 million dollar seed round from Cherry Ventures and an 8 million dollar round led by Target Global with Zalando participating.

use_cases.description: Development agencies and in-house engineering teams build fully custom, high-performance storefronts on top of Saleor's GraphQL API.

use_cases.title: Composable commerce migration
use_cases.description: Mid-market and enterprise retailers move off monolithic platforms to a MACH-compliant, API-first commerce backend.

use_cases.description: Merchants selling across multiple channels, brands, or currencies use Saleor's channel model to manage them from one backend.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based sales commission and incentive compensation management software for automating commission calculations, plan design, and payout reporting.
enrichment.seo_title: Sales Cookie Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sales Cookie pricing, features, and plans for sales commission automation. See how this incentive compensation management tool works and what it costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sales Cookie is a cloud-based sales commission and incentive compensation management platform designed to help small and mid-sized businesses automate the calculation, tracking, and payout of sales commissions without relying on error-prone spreadsheets. The product is operated by Ninth Floor Technologies and is headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, in the United States; the company describes itself as 100 percent cloud-based and 100 percent built in the USA. Public materials indicate the platform has maintained the same per-seat pricing for existing customers for roughly eight years as of 2026, with customers who signed up around 2018 still paying that original rate, which places its market launch in approximately 2018. Sales Cookie is sold on a pay-as-you-go, per-user monthly subscription with no long-term contracts and no setup fees; a 'user' in its pricing model covers both commission-earning payees and plan administrators. The entry point is a 14-day free trial with full feature access using sample demo data and no credit card required. Paid tiers include a Business plan at 40 dollars per user per month, the most commonly selected option, which includes AI-assisted plan configuration, a visual plan designer, real-time commission dashboards for reps and managers, manual adjustment tools, team management, enterprise-grade reporting, integrations with CRM and other business systems, multi-currency support, and audit logs. A higher Business+ tier at 60 dollars per user per month is aimed at more demanding organizations, supporting 25 or more concurrent commission plans, over 100,000 transactions, and over 1,000 calculations, and adds custom authentication, custom integrations, white-labeling, API access, and priority support and onboarding. Sales Cookie also offers free plan configuration for a customer's first incentive plan regardless of tier, and extends a full year of free access to qualifying early-stage technology startups with under 500,000 dollars in annual revenue. The platform is positioned to replace manual commission tracking in spreadsheets for sales organizations of varying sizes, competing in the incentive compensation management category alongside tools such as CaptivateIQ, Xactly, and QuotaPath. Because it emphasizes transparent, published per-user pricing, no long-term commitment, and free onboarding for the first commission plan, Sales Cookie is frequently positioned toward small and mid-market sales teams that want to move off spreadsheets quickly without a lengthy procurement or implementation process, distinguishing it from more enterprise-oriented incentive compensation platforms that require custom sales-led pricing and longer deployment timelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sales Cookie is a commission automation platform operated by Ninth Floor Technologies and headquartered in Bellevue, Washington. It focuses on replacing manual, spreadsheet-based commission tracking with automated calculations, dashboards, and audit trails for sales teams and their managers.

The company markets itself as a fully cloud-based, US-built platform, and has kept consistent per-seat pricing for long-tenured customers since roughly 2018, positioning itself as a straightforward, transparently priced alternative to larger enterprise incentive compensation suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sales Cookie centers on a visual, AI-assisted plan designer for building commission structures, paired with real-time dashboards that let reps and managers track earnings and quota attainment as deals close.

Additional capabilities include manual adjustment workflows, enterprise-grade reporting, multi-currency support, audit logs for compliance, and integrations that pull deal and payment data from CRM and other business systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sales Cookie is sold on a pay-as-you-go, per-user monthly basis with no long-term contract required. The Business plan costs 40 dollars per user per month and covers most core functionality, while Business+ at 60 dollars per user per month adds higher usage limits, custom integrations, white-labeling, and API access.

New customers get a 14-day free trial with full feature access, and every customer's first incentive plan is configured free of charge; qualifying early-stage startups under 500,000 dollars in revenue can use the platform free for a full year.

features.description: Reporting tools for finance and sales operations teams to audit and analyze commission spend.

features.title: CRM and system integrations
features.description: Connects to CRM and other business systems to pull deal and payment data automatically.

features.title: Multi-currency support
features.description: Calculates and displays commissions across multiple currencies for global sales teams.

features.title: Audit logs
features.description: Tracks changes to plans and calculations for compliance and dispute resolution.

features.title: Free first plan configuration
features.description: Sales Cookie configures a customer's first commission plan at no charge as part of onboarding.

pros: Transparent, published per-user pricing with no long-term contract required
Free trial and free first-plan configuration lower the barrier to getting started
Consistent pricing for long-tenured customers since around 2018
Free year of access for qualifying early-stage startups
Covers core incentive compensation management needs without enterprise-grade complexity
cons: Company size and broader corporate information are not widely published
Higher usage limits and API access require the pricier Business+ tier
May lack the depth of customization larger enterprise incentive compensation suites offer
Per-user pricing can add up for organizations with many payees
Less brand recognition than larger competitors like Xactly or CaptivateIQ

faqs.question: What is Sales Cookie used for?
faqs.answer: Sales Cookie automates sales commission and incentive compensation calculations, dashboards, and payout tracking, replacing manual spreadsheet-based processes.

faqs.question: How much does Sales Cookie cost?
faqs.answer: The Business plan costs 40 dollars per user per month, and the Business+ plan costs 60 dollars per user per month, both billed on a pay-as-you-go monthly basis.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Sales Cookie?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sales Cookie offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access using sample demo data and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Who owns Sales Cookie?
faqs.answer: Sales Cookie is operated by Ninth Floor Technologies, headquartered in Bellevue, Washington, United States.

faqs.question: Does Sales Cookie require a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: No, Sales Cookie is sold on a pay-as-you-go monthly basis with no long-term commitment required.

faqs.question: Does Sales Cookie offer discounts for startups?
faqs.answer: Yes, qualifying early-stage technology startups with under 500,000 dollars in annual revenue can use Sales Cookie free for one year.

faqs.question: Does Sales Cookie integrate with CRM systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sales Cookie connects to CRM and other business systems to automatically pull deal and payment data for commission calculations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Business and Business+ plans?
faqs.answer: Business+ supports higher volumes (25-plus plans, 100,000-plus transactions), and adds custom authentication, custom integrations, white-labeling, and API access on top of the Business plan.

enrichment.short_description: Quote-to-cash platform for B2B SaaS companies, combining quoting, contracts and e-signature, billing, and revenue provisioning into one self-serve checkout system.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription with fees based on invoiced transaction volume
enrichment.seo_title: Salesbricks Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salesbricks pricing, features, and plans for B2B SaaS quote-to-cash. See how this quoting, billing, and contract platform works and what it costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesbricks is a quote-to-cash platform purpose-built for B2B SaaS companies, unifying pricing and quoting, contract creation and e-signature, billing, and payment collection into a single self-serve checkout workflow. The company was founded in 2021 by John Louis Swaine and Jon Festejo and is headquartered in San Mateo, California. According to public company data, Salesbricks has around 29 employees and has raised approximately 18.3 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Surface Ventures and Long Journey Ventures. The product targets revenue and go-to-market teams at software companies that want to collapse the traditionally fragmented quote-to-cash process, which often spans separate CPQ, e-signature, billing, and CRM tools, into one connected system. Core capabilities include a configurable library of legal terms such as Master Service Agreements, Commercial Terms, Data Processing Agreements, Service Level Agreements, and Legal Exhibits that can be attached to deals so contracts are standardized and ready to sign; usage-based and standard quoting and billing; a full API that lets engineering teams programmatically control the revenue workflow; and integrations that connect CRM, accounting, and other internal systems. Rather than charging a flat software fee alone, Salesbricks' pricing model combines tiered monthly subscription plans with a fee based on the value of invoices generated through the platform, meaning the effective cost scales with a customer's transaction volume. Published pricing includes a Startup plan at 500 dollars per month billed annually, supporting up to 5 app users, limited reporting and analytics, and up to 300,000 dollars in transaction volume, with additional transaction capacity available in 500,000 dollar increments; a Growth plan at 1,500 dollars per month billed annually, which removes the user limit, adds full analytics, a dedicated support manager, and scoped migration assistance, and covers 300,000 dollars to 2 million dollars in transaction volume; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan with unlimited users, full analytics, CRM and ERP integrations, and a dedicated customer success manager for larger transaction volumes. Salesbricks also references negotiable or alternative subscription arrangements for companies that want to discuss rates directly with its sales team. In the market, Salesbricks is typically compared to other quote-to-cash and CPQ platforms such as Alguna, and it is positioned as a modern, API-first alternative aimed specifically at B2B SaaS companies rather than the broader, more generalized enterprise CPQ or billing suites used across industries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesbricks is a quote-to-cash platform for B2B SaaS companies, founded in 2021 by John Louis Swaine and Jon Festejo and headquartered in San Mateo, California. It combines quoting, contracts, e-signature, billing, and payment collection into a single connected workflow.

The company has raised roughly 18.3 million dollars from investors including Surface Ventures and Long Journey Ventures and operates with a team of around 29 employees, positioning it as a venture-backed, API-first alternative to fragmented quote-to-cash tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Salesbricks provides a configurable library of legal terms, including Master Service Agreements, Commercial Terms, Data Processing Agreements, Service Level Agreements, and Legal Exhibits, that can be attached to deals for standardized, compliant contracts.

The platform also supports usage-based and standard quoting and billing, offers a full API for programmatic control of the revenue workflow, and integrates with CRM, accounting, and other internal systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Salesbricks combines tiered monthly subscription pricing with fees tied to the value of invoices processed through the platform. The Startup plan is 500 dollars per month billed annually for up to 5 users and 300,000 dollars in transaction volume, while Growth is 1,500 dollars per month billed annually with unlimited users and up to 2 million dollars in transaction volume.

Larger customers move to a custom-priced Enterprise plan with unlimited users, full analytics, and CRM and ERP integrations; additional transaction capacity beyond each plan's included volume is available in 500,000 dollar increments.

features.title: Unified quote-to-cash workflow
features.description: Combines quoting, contracts, e-signature, billing, and payment collection in one self-serve checkout system.

features.title: Configurable legal terms library
features.description: Standardized Master Service Agreements, Commercial Terms, Data Processing Agreements, Service Level Agreements, and Legal Exhibits attachable to deals.

features.title: Usage-based and standard billing
features.description: Supports both flat and usage-based pricing and billing models common to B2B SaaS.

features.title: Full API access
features.description: Lets engineering teams programmatically control every part of the revenue workflow.

features.title: CRM and ERP integrations
features.description: Connects to CRM, accounting, and other internal systems to keep revenue data in sync.

features.title: Self-serve checkout
features.description: Enables buyers to review, sign, and pay for contracts without manual sales-ops intervention.

features.title: Dedicated support and migration assistance
features.description: Growth and Enterprise plans include a dedicated support manager and scoped migration help.

pros: Unifies quoting, contracts, billing, and payments into one platform instead of stitching together multiple tools
Purpose-built for B2B SaaS revenue workflows rather than generic enterprise CPQ
Full API access for engineering-led, programmatic revenue operations
Dedicated support and migration assistance included on Growth and Enterprise plans
Backed by venture funding, suggesting ongoing product investment
cons: Pricing combines subscription fees with transaction-based fees, which can be harder to predict than flat SaaS pricing
Entry-level Startup plan caps app users at 5
Full feature set and better rates often require negotiating directly with sales
Relatively small team and young company compared to established billing platforms
Detailed public pricing beyond the three published tiers is limited

faqs.question: What does Salesbricks do?
faqs.answer: Salesbricks is a quote-to-cash platform for B2B SaaS companies that combines quoting, contracts and e-signature, billing, and payment collection into one system.

faqs.question: How much does Salesbricks cost?
faqs.answer: The Startup plan is 500 dollars per month billed annually, the Growth plan is 1,500 dollars per month billed annually, and Enterprise pricing is custom, with additional fees tied to invoiced transaction volume.

faqs.question: When was Salesbricks founded?
faqs.answer: Salesbricks was founded in 2021 by John Louis Swaine and Jon Festejo.

faqs.question: Where is Salesbricks headquartered?
faqs.answer: Salesbricks is headquartered in San Mateo, California, United States.

faqs.question: Does Salesbricks charge transaction fees?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to its tiered subscription plans, Salesbricks charges a fee based on the value of invoices generated through the platform.

faqs.question: Does Salesbricks integrate with CRM systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Salesbricks integrates with CRM, ERP, and accounting systems, with CRM integration included from the Startup plan and CRM plus ERP integration on Enterprise.

faqs.question: How much funding has Salesbricks raised?
faqs.answer: Salesbricks has raised approximately 18.3 million dollars from investors including Surface Ventures and Long Journey Ventures.

faqs.question: Who is Salesbricks best suited for?
faqs.answer: Salesbricks is best suited for B2B SaaS companies that want to unify quoting, contracting, billing, and payment collection into a single self-serve checkout system.

enrichment.short_description: Intelligent CRM built for small and medium B2B businesses that automates data entry by pulling activity from email, calendar, LinkedIn, and phone.
enrichment.seo_title: Salesflare Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salesflare CRM pricing, features, and plans for 2026. See how this automated, LinkedIn-integrated CRM for small B2B teams works and what it costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesflare is a CRM platform built specifically for small and medium-sized B2B businesses, with a core focus on automating the manual data entry that typically burdens sales reps. The company was founded in 2014 in Antwerp, Belgium by Jeroen Corthout and Lieven Janssen, and remains headquartered in Antwerp, with an additional office in New York; it operates as a lean, product-focused company with roughly 30 employees. Rather than requiring reps to manually log emails, meetings, and calls, Salesflare automatically captures communication from Gmail, Outlook, LinkedIn, and phone activity, building contact timelines and deal histories without manual input, and enriches contact records using data pulled from email signatures, LinkedIn profiles, and company websites. Its native LinkedIn integration, available on all plans, connects with both personal LinkedIn accounts and LinkedIn Sales Navigator through a Chrome extension, letting users add prospects, retrieve verified business email addresses, and update CRM records directly from LinkedIn. The platform also includes email and link tracking, automated follow-up reminders, and relationship intelligence that scores connection strength based on email activity patterns, alongside visual pipeline management and integrations with Gmail, Outlook, LinkedIn, and Zapier. Salesflare is sold on a three-tier, per-user subscription model with a 30-day free trial, no setup fees, and optional add-on lead credits for its built-in email finder. The Growth plan starts at 29 dollars per user per month when billed annually (39 dollars billed monthly), the Pro plan is 49 dollars per user per month annually (64 dollars monthly), and the Enterprise plan is 99 dollars per user per month annually (124 dollars monthly) with a five-user minimum. Salesflare is commonly positioned against CRMs like Pipedrive, HubSpot, and Copper, and is frequently recommended for small B2B teams, agencies, and startups that want a CRM that keeps itself updated automatically rather than one that requires disciplined manual logging, which the company argues improves data completeness and adoption compared to traditional CRMs. Its focus on automation, relationship intelligence, and LinkedIn-native prospecting differentiates it from larger, more configurable enterprise CRMs, at the tradeoff of less deep customization for complex, multi-department sales organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesflare is a CRM built for small and medium B2B businesses, founded in 2014 in Antwerp, Belgium by Jeroen Corthout and Lieven Janssen. The company remains headquartered in Antwerp with an additional office in New York, and operates with a lean team of around 30 employees.

Its defining focus is automating CRM data entry, using email, calendar, LinkedIn, and phone activity to automatically build contact timelines and deal histories, reducing the manual logging that causes adoption problems in traditional CRMs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Salesflare automatically captures communication and enriches contact records from email signatures, LinkedIn profiles, and company websites, and includes native LinkedIn and LinkedIn Sales Navigator integration on every plan through a Chrome extension.

Other core features include email and link tracking, automated follow-up reminders, relationship intelligence scoring based on email activity, visual pipeline management, and integrations with Gmail, Outlook, and Zapier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Salesflare offers three per-user pricing tiers with a 30-day free trial and no setup fees: Growth at 29 dollars per user per month billed annually, Pro at 49 dollars per user per month billed annually, and Enterprise at 99 dollars per user per month billed annually with a five-user minimum.
Monthly billing is available at higher rates (39, 64, and 124 dollars per user per month respectively), and optional add-on lead credits can be purchased for the built-in email finder.

Best suited to email-centric B2B sales motions rather than all industries

enrichment.seo_title: Salesforce Review 2026: Sales Cloud Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salesforce Sales Cloud pricing, plans, and features for 2026, from the 25 dollar Starter Suite to the Agentforce 1 Sales tier. See what each edition includes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesforce is the customer relationship management platform founded in 1999 by Marc Benioff, Parker Harris, Dave Moellenhoff, and Frank Dominguez, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It pioneered the software-as-a-service delivery model for enterprise applications and has grown into the world's largest CRM provider by market share, reporting 41.5 billion dollars in revenue for its 2026 fiscal year, up 10 percent year over year, and employing more than 83,000 people globally as a publicly traded company (NYSE: CRM). Sales Cloud is Salesforce's original and best-known product, providing lead and opportunity management, pipeline and forecasting tools, workflow automation, and reporting for sales teams, and today it spans five main pricing editions. Starter Suite costs 25 dollars per user per month and is the only edition available on monthly billing, bundling basic sales, service, marketing, and commerce tools along with Slack at no extra cost, aimed at small businesses. Pro Suite steps up to 100 dollars per user per month, adding deeper customization, sales automation, and reporting for growing teams. Enterprise, at 175 dollars per user per month, is where most mid-market sales organizations land, unlocking advanced automation, territory management, and the API access needed for deeper systems integration. Unlimited, at 350 dollars per user per month, adds the full platform feature set along with premium support and higher usage limits. The top tier, Agentforce 1 Sales, costs 550 dollars per user per month billed annually and bundles the Unlimited feature set with Salesforce's full AI agent suite, unmetered employee Agentforce usage, Slack Enterprise Plus, Spiff incentive compensation, Sales Planning, Sales Programs, Maps, Tableau Next, and org-level AI credits. With the exception of Starter Suite, all Sales Cloud editions are sold on annual contracts paid in advance rather than month-to-month. Salesforce's broader platform extends well beyond Sales Cloud into products such as Service Cloud for customer support, Marketing Cloud, Commerce Cloud, Slack, Tableau, MuleSoft, and the Data Cloud and Agentforce AI layer that now underpins many of its newer offerings. The company is known for a vast partner and consulting ecosystem, an extensive AppExchange marketplace of third-party integrations, and its annual Dreamforce conference. Because of its depth of customization and scalability, Salesforce Sales Cloud is most commonly chosen by mid-market and enterprise sales organizations that need extensive configurability, though its per-user pricing and add-on costs can make it considerably more expensive than lighter-weight CRMs aimed at small businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesforce was founded in 1999 by Marc Benioff, Parker Harris, Dave Moellenhoff, and Frank Dominguez, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. It pioneered cloud-delivered CRM software and has grown into the world's largest CRM vendor, reporting 41.5 billion dollars in revenue for fiscal year 2026 and employing more than 83,000 people worldwide.

Sales Cloud is Salesforce's flagship product for managing leads, opportunities, pipelines, and sales forecasting, and forms the foundation of a much broader platform that now includes Service Cloud, Marketing Cloud, Slack, Tableau, and the Agentforce AI layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sales Cloud provides lead and opportunity management, customizable pipelines, sales forecasting, workflow automation, and reporting and dashboards, with deep customization available through its underlying platform (formerly known as Lightning Platform or Force.com).

Higher tiers add territory management, advanced automation, API access for integrations, and increasingly, embedded generative AI through Salesforce's Agentforce agents, which can automate tasks like lead qualification, follow-ups, and forecasting analysis.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sales Cloud is priced per user per month across five editions: Starter Suite at 25 dollars, Pro Suite at 100 dollars, Enterprise at 175 dollars, Unlimited at 350 dollars, and Agentforce 1 Sales at 550 dollars, the last four billed annually.

Starter Suite is the only edition offering monthly billing; all other editions require an annual contract paid in advance, and add-ons such as additional Agentforce credits, extra storage, or premium support can increase the effective per-user cost.

features.description: Automates repetitive sales tasks, approvals, and follow-ups using built-in automation tools.

features.description: A large third-party marketplace of apps and integrations that extend Sales Cloud functionality.

features.title: Territory and API access
features.description: Enterprise-tier and above unlock territory management and API access for system integration.

features.title: Agentforce AI agents
features.description: Higher tiers include embedded AI agents that can automate sales tasks and generate insights.

pros: Extremely deep customization and scalability suited to complex sales organizations
Massive third-party ecosystem through the AppExchange marketplace
Wide partner and consulting network for implementation support
Continual investment in AI capabilities through Agentforce
Integrates natively with the broader Salesforce platform, including Service Cloud, Slack, and Tableau
cons: Per-user pricing and required add-ons can make total cost significantly higher than lighter CRMs
All editions except Starter Suite require annual contracts paid upfront
Implementation and customization often require paid consulting or admin expertise
Steep learning curve for administrators configuring advanced features
Top-tier AI-inclusive plan, Agentforce 1 Sales, is priced well above competitors' comparable offerings

faqs.question: How much does Salesforce Sales Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Sales Cloud pricing runs from 25 dollars per user per month for Starter Suite up to 550 dollars per user per month for the Agentforce 1 Sales edition, with Pro Suite, Enterprise, and Unlimited in between.

faqs.question: When was Salesforce founded?
faqs.answer: Salesforce was founded in 1999 by Marc Benioff, Parker Harris, Dave Moellenhoff, and Frank Dominguez.

faqs.question: Where is Salesforce headquartered?
faqs.answer: Salesforce is headquartered in San Francisco, California, United States.

faqs.question: Can I pay for Salesforce monthly?
faqs.answer: Only the Starter Suite edition offers monthly billing; all other Sales Cloud editions require an annual contract paid in advance.

faqs.question: How big is Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Salesforce employs more than 83,000 people worldwide and reported 41.5 billion dollars in revenue for its 2026 fiscal year.

faqs.answer: Starter Suite bundles basic sales, service, marketing, and commerce tools along with Slack at no extra cost, aimed at small businesses.

faqs.question: What is Agentforce 1 Sales?
faqs.answer: Agentforce 1 Sales is Salesforce's top Sales Cloud edition, bundling the Unlimited feature set with unmetered AI agent usage, Slack Enterprise Plus, Spiff, Sales Planning, and Tableau Next.

faqs.answer: No, Sales Cloud focuses on sales pipeline and opportunity management, while Service Cloud is Salesforce's separate product for customer service and support.

enrichment.seo_title: Salesforce Service Cloud Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Salesforce Service Cloud pricing, plans, and features for 2026, from the 25 dollar Starter Suite to the 500 dollar Einstein 1 Service tier.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesforce Service Cloud is the customer service and support product line of Salesforce, Inc., the CRM company founded in 1999 by Marc Benioff, Parker Harris, Dave Moellenhoff, and Frank Dominguez, and headquartered in San Francisco, California, with more than 83,000 employees worldwide as of its fiscal 2026 reporting. While Salesforce itself dates to 1999, Service Cloud as a distinct product was launched in 2009 to bring case management and customer support automation into the same platform as Sales Cloud, and it has since been substantially expanded with AI capabilities, most notably through Einstein, which Salesforce introduced at its Dreamforce conference in September 2016 and later extended with generative AI through Einstein GPT in 2023. Service Cloud is built around case management, omnichannel routing that sends incoming customer inquiries to the most qualified available agent across channels like email, chat, phone, and social, a knowledge base for self-service and agent-assisted resolution, and a service console that gives agents a unified workspace for handling cases. Its Einstein AI layer adds Einstein Bots for automated conversational support, automatic case classification, and AI-generated reply recommendations, all running on Salesforce's Einstein Trust Layer, which is designed to keep sensitive customer data from being stored inside third-party large language models and to support GDPR and CCPA compliance. Service Cloud is priced per user per month across the same five-tier structure as Sales Cloud: Starter Suite at 25 dollars, Pro Suite at 100 dollars, Enterprise at 165 dollars, Unlimited at 330 dollars, and the top AI-inclusive tier, Einstein 1 Service, at 500 dollars. As with Sales Cloud, Starter Suite is the only edition available on monthly billing; every other tier is sold as a twelve-month annual commitment. Service Cloud carries some of the heaviest optional add-on costs in the Salesforce portfolio, since capabilities like Digital Engagement (messaging channels), Service Cloud Voice (telephony integration), Field Service (dispatch and mobile workforce management), and expanded Knowledge Base functionality are each priced separately, typically adding 50 to 200 dollars per user per month on top of the base edition. Because of this modular add-on structure, real-world Service Cloud costs for a fully equipped contact center are often considerably higher than the base per-user list price suggests. Service Cloud is commonly evaluated against dedicated help desk and support platforms such as Zendesk, Freshdesk, and Intercom, and is most often chosen by organizations that already use or plan to use Salesforce Sales Cloud and want unified sales and service data on one CRM platform rather than a standalone support tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesforce Service Cloud is the customer service and support product of Salesforce, Inc., founded in 1999 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Service Cloud itself launched in 2009 as Salesforce's dedicated platform for case management and support automation, sitting alongside Sales Cloud within the broader Salesforce ecosystem.

The product has been significantly expanded with AI, starting with Einstein in 2016 and generative AI through Einstein GPT in 2023, and today ranges from a 25 dollar per user per month Starter Suite up to a 500 dollar per user per month Einstein 1 Service tier with embedded generative AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core Service Cloud functionality centers on case management, omnichannel routing that directs inquiries to the best-available agent across channels, a knowledge base for self-service, and a unified service console workspace for agents.

Its Einstein AI layer adds Einstein Bots, automated case classification, and AI-generated reply recommendations, all governed by the Einstein Trust Layer to help keep sensitive data out of third-party LLMs and support GDPR and CCPA compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Service Cloud is priced per user per month across five editions: Starter Suite at 25 dollars, Pro Suite at 100 dollars, Enterprise at 165 dollars, Unlimited at 330 dollars, and Einstein 1 Service at 500 dollars, with only Starter Suite available on monthly billing.

Add-on modules such as Digital Engagement, Service Cloud Voice, Field Service, and expanded Knowledge Base functionality are priced separately, often adding 50 to 200 dollars per user per month, so a fully equipped contact center typically costs more than the base list price.

Strong omnichannel routing and case management for complex support organizations
Deep AI investment through Einstein and Einstein 1 Service with a dedicated trust and compliance layer
Highly configurable service console and workflow automation
Backed by Salesforce's large partner, consulting, and AppExchange ecosystem
cons: Add-on modules like Voice, Field Service, and Digital Engagement significantly increase real-world cost beyond base list pricing
All editions except Starter Suite require annual contracts paid upfront
Implementation and configuration often require paid Salesforce consulting expertise
Pricing at the Enterprise and above tiers is considerably higher than dedicated help desk tools like Zendesk or Freshdesk
Steep learning curve for administrators managing complex omnichannel and AI configurations

faqs.question: How much does Salesforce Service Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Service Cloud pricing runs from 25 dollars per user per month for Starter Suite up to 500 dollars per user per month for the Einstein 1 Service edition, with Pro Suite, Enterprise, and Unlimited in between.

faqs.question: When did Salesforce Service Cloud launch?
faqs.answer: Service Cloud launched in 2009 as Salesforce's dedicated customer service and case management platform.

faqs.question: What is Einstein 1 Service?
faqs.answer: Einstein 1 Service is Service Cloud's top tier, bundling the Unlimited feature set with embedded generative AI, unmetered AI usage, and Data Cloud capabilities, priced at 500 dollars per user per month.

faqs.question: Does Service Cloud include phone support?
faqs.answer: Basic channels are included in core Service Cloud, but full telephony integration requires the separately priced Service Cloud Voice add-on.

faqs.question: Is Service Cloud the same as Sales Cloud?
faqs.answer: No, Service Cloud is Salesforce's separate product for customer support and case management, while Sales Cloud focuses on sales pipeline and opportunity management, though both share the same underlying Salesforce platform.

faqs.question: Can I pay for Service Cloud monthly?
faqs.answer: Only the Starter Suite edition offers monthly billing; all higher editions require an annual contract paid in advance.

faqs.question: What AI features does Service Cloud include?
faqs.answer: Service Cloud includes Einstein Bots, automated case classification, and AI-generated reply recommendations, running on the Einstein Trust Layer for data protection and compliance.

faqs.question: Who makes Salesforce Service Cloud?
faqs.answer: Service Cloud is made by Salesforce, Inc., founded in 1999 and headquartered in San Francisco, California.

enrichment.seo_title: Saleshandy Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Saleshandy pricing, features, and plans for 2026. See how this cold email outreach and lead generation platform works and what each tier costs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Saleshandy is a cold email outreach and lead generation platform used by sales teams, agencies, and recruiters to run multi-step email sequences, find and verify prospect email addresses, and manage sender deliverability at scale. The company was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India, operating as a private SaaS company with an estimated 50 to 100 employees and an additional US-registered presence in Newark, Delaware. Saleshandy states it is trusted by more than 25,000 businesses, agencies, and sales teams worldwide. The platform's core capabilities include unlimited email account connections, automated multi-step sequences with conditional logic, email warmup tools to protect sender reputation and inbox placement, a built-in email finder and verifier for building prospect lists, personalization at scale, A/B testing of subject lines and copy, and analytics dashboards for tracking open, reply, and bounce rates across campaigns. Saleshandy is sold on a tiered monthly subscription model based primarily on sending volume, with all published tiers offering a lower rate on annual billing versus monthly billing. The Outreach Starter plan costs 25 dollars per month on annual billing and supports sending up to 6,000 emails per month, aimed at freelancers, startups, and small businesses just beginning cold outreach. The Outreach Pro plan costs 69 dollars per month on annual billing and raises the sending limit to 100,000 emails per month while adding enhanced personalization and A/B testing, aimed at scaling teams and sales professionals. The Outreach Scale plan costs 139 dollars per month on annual billing with higher sending volume and deliverability tooling. The top published tier, Outreach Scale Plus, costs 209 dollars per month on annual billing (or 299 dollars per user per month on monthly billing) and supports up to 300,000 emails per month with AI-powered automation and premium support, targeted at large-scale operations, agencies, and enterprises. Because Saleshandy centers specifically on cold email deliverability and sequencing rather than being a full CRM, it is most commonly compared against other cold outreach tools such as Instantly, Smartlead, Lemlist, and Woodpecker rather than against general-purpose sales engagement platforms. Its combination of unlimited email accounts even on lower tiers, built-in lead-finding data, and warmup tooling positions it as a cost-conscious option for teams running high-volume outbound email campaigns who want deliverability and prospecting bundled into one subscription rather than paid separately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Saleshandy is a cold email outreach platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in Ahmedabad, Gujarat, India, with an estimated 50 to 100 employees and a US presence registered in Newark, Delaware. It serves sales teams, agencies, and recruiters running high-volume outbound email campaigns.

The company reports being used by more than 25,000 businesses, agencies, and sales teams worldwide, and centers its product on combining email sequencing, deliverability, and prospect data finding in one subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include unlimited email account connections, automated multi-step sequences with conditional logic, email warmup to protect sender reputation, and a built-in email finder and verifier for building prospect lists.
Higher tiers add enhanced personalization, A/B testing of subject lines and copy, deliverability tooling, AI-powered automation, and analytics dashboards for tracking open, reply, and bounce rates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Saleshandy is priced on sending volume across four Outreach tiers billed annually: Starter at 25 dollars per month (up to 6,000 emails/month), Pro at 69 dollars per month (up to 100,000 emails/month), Scale at 139 dollars per month, and Scale Plus at 209 dollars per month (up to 300,000 emails/month).
Monthly billing is available at higher rates, with Scale Plus costing 299 dollars per user per month when billed monthly instead of annually.

faqs.answer: No, Saleshandy is focused specifically on cold email outreach and lead generation rather than being a full CRM.

use_cases.title: Cold email outreach campaigns
use_cases.description: Sales teams use Saleshandy to build and automate multi-step cold email sequences to prospects.
use_cases.audience: Sales development reps and outbound sales teams

use_cases.title: Agency client prospecting
use_cases.description: Agencies run outreach campaigns on behalf of clients using Saleshandy's sequencing and deliverability tools.

use_cases.title: Recruiter candidate sourcing
use_cases.description: Recruiters use email finding and sequencing to reach out to candidates at scale.
